---
title: "Manuscript Preparation & Peer Review"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Advanced]{.level-badge .level-advanced} · **Communication & Dissemination** track · ~35 min hands-on + readings and quiz
:::

[← Back to the Communication & Dissemination track](communication.qmd){.track-back}

*Use AI to prepare manuscripts and assist review — within strict confidentiality and disclosure rules.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Draft and tighten manuscript sections, abstracts, and reviewer responses
- Apply reporting checklists (STROBE/CONSORT) with AI help
- Respect confidentiality and journal AI policies in peer review

## Overview

AI can draft a methods paragraph, tighten a results narrative, structure a cover letter, and organize point-by-point reviewer responses. The analysis, interpretation, and authorship remain yours, and AI use is typically disclosed.

Peer review has a hard rule: a manuscript you're reviewing is **confidential**. Do not paste an unpublished manuscript into an AI tool, and follow each journal's policy on AI in review — many prohibit it outright.

## Practice activities

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 1 · Advanced — Tighten a section against a reporting checklist</summary>
```

**Time** ~18 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu

**Goal.** Use a reporting guideline to strengthen your own methods section.

**Setup.** Your own draft methods section (non-sensitive) and the relevant checklist (e.g., STROBE for observational studies).

**Steps.**

1. Find the gaps:

   > Here is my methods section and the STROBE checklist. Which checklist items are missing, unclear, or under-reported? Be specific. [methods] [checklist]

2. Revise to address them; confirm each change is true to what you actually did.

3. Improve reproducibility:

   > Suggest where I should add detail to make this reproducible, without padding.

**Expected result.** A checklist-aligned methods section.

**Check your work.** Every added detail must match your real methods — don’t let AI invent procedures you didn’t use.

**Common pitfalls.** AI “improves” by adding things you didn’t do. You own accuracy. Use an approved tool for unpublished work.

**Stretch (optional).** Generate a STROBE/CONSORT compliance table to submit with the paper.

```{=html}
</details>
```

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 2 · Advanced — Structure a response to reviewers</summary>
```

**Time** ~15 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu

**Goal.** Organize a clear, point-by-point reviewer response — while respecting confidentiality.

**Setup.** Use this fictional methods excerpt and reviewer comments (or substitute your own). **Never paste a manuscript you are reviewing for a journal.**

**Fictional methods paragraph:**

> We conducted a cross-sectional analysis of pedestrian crashes in Philadelphia from 2018–2023 using PennDOT crash records linked to road-segment characteristics from OpenStreetMap. Fatal and serious injuries (KSI) were modeled using logistic regression. Covariates included lane count, posted speed limit, crosswalk presence, and lighting. Census-tract income quartile was included as a fixed effect. We excluded crashes on highways and those involving intoxicated pedestrians. Missing speed-limit data (18% of segments) were imputed using the median of adjacent segments.

**Fictional reviewer comments:**

> **Reviewer 1:**
>
> - Major: The exclusion of intoxicated pedestrians is unexplained and potentially introduces selection bias. Please justify or conduct a sensitivity analysis including these cases.
> - Major: Imputing speed limits using the median of adjacent segments is ad hoc. How sensitive are results to this choice? Consider a sensitivity analysis with complete cases only.
> - Minor: Table 2 header says “OR” but the footnote says “adjusted OR” — which is it?
>
> **Reviewer 2:**
>
> - Major: The cross-sectional design limits causal claims, but the discussion reads as if these are causal findings (“reducing lanes prevents KSI”). Reframe.
> - Minor: Why OpenStreetMap rather than PennDOT’s road inventory? Address data-quality concerns.
> - Minor: Line 142 — “significant” used without a p-value or CI.

**Steps.**

1. Organize the response:

   > Organize these reviewer comments and my planned responses into a professional, point-by-point response letter grouped by reviewer, with polite framing. For each comment, acknowledge the concern, describe the revision, and note the page/line where the change was made. Reviewer comments: [paste above]. My planned responses: Reviewer 1 Major 1 — we will add a sensitivity analysis including intoxicated pedestrians. R1 Major 2 — we will add a complete-case sensitivity analysis. R1 Minor — it’s adjusted OR; we’ll fix the header. R2 Major — we’ll reframe to associational language. R2 Minor 1 — we chose OSM for coverage; we’ll add a data-quality note. R2 Minor 2 — we’ll add the CI.

2. Verify every “we have revised…” statement: do you actually plan to make each change? Don’t promise a revision you won’t do.

3. Soften where needed:

   > Flag any response that sounds defensive and suggest a more constructive phrasing.

**Expected result.** A clean, professional response-to-reviewers letter organized by reviewer, with polite framing that acknowledges each concern before describing the revision.

**Check your work.** Confirm each claim of a change matches what you’ll actually revise. The most common error is promising a sensitivity analysis you don’t actually run.

**Common pitfalls.** Hard rule: never put a manuscript you’re peer-reviewing into an AI tool — it’s confidential and many journals prohibit it. Disclose AI use in your own writing per ICMJE guidance.

**Stretch (optional).** Draft a short cover letter to the editor summarizing the three major revisions.

```{=html}
</details>
```


## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "A manuscript you're peer-reviewing is…", "options": ["Confidential — don't put it in an AI tool", "Fine to paste anywhere", "Public", "Yours to share"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Review confidentiality is a firm rule; many journals prohibit AI in review."}, {"q": "In your own manuscript, AI use is typically…", "options": ["Disclosed per journal/ICMJE guidance", "Hidden", "Required", "Irrelevant"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Disclose AI assistance; AI can't be an author."}, {"q": "What does AI not do for you here?", "options": ["Own the analysis, interpretation, and authorship", "Draft text", "Tighten language", "Format a letter"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Substance and authorship stay with you."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

Available in the shared OneDrive folder **Staff Faculty AI Workshop → Readings**, and online where linked:

- [**Peer Review Is Dead; Long Live Peer Review!**](https://daafguide.substack.com/p/a-better-ai-future-for-peer-review) — how AI is reshaping the review system.
- [**AI-Slop, GrantaGate and Bad Writing**](https://tuhinchakrabarty.substack.com/p/ai-slop-grantagate-and-bad-writing) — cautionary view of AI-generated academic text.

## Useful resources

- [ICMJE Recommendations](https://www.icmje.org) — authorship and AI-disclosure guidance.
- [EQUATOR Network](https://www.equator-network.org) — reporting guidelines (STROBE, CONSORT, etc.).
- [ChatGPT Edu (Drexel AI Tools)](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools) — the approved tool, with data rules.

---

## Navigate

**Next in this track:** [Writing Your Course AI Policy](course-ai-policy.qmd)

**Related topics:** [Literature Review & Evidence Synthesis](literature-review.qmd) · [Verifying AI Output](verifying-output.qmd)

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
**Never** paste a manuscript you're reviewing into any AI tool; use approved tools for your own drafts and disclose AI use. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
